title: Motivational interviewing: explore the patient's ambivalence
vignette: A 24-year-old man comes to the office because of mood changes. He has felt depressed, irritable, and anxious nearly every day for 2 months. The patient has difficulty getting out of bed in the mornings and staying focused at work. He has no medical conditions and takes no medications. The patient drinks 3 or 4 beers daily and more on weekends. The physician provides education about the link between alcohol and mood changes. The patient responds, "But it really helps me to unwind and relax after a long workday and makes it easier to fall asleep. It's also how I hang out with my friends on weekends. I can't see quitting." Which of the following is the most appropriate statement by the physician at this time?

clues.text: drinks 3 or 4 beers daily and more on weekends
clues.note: Daily heavy drinking contributing to depressed/anxious mood sets up a behavior-change conversation. The patient is the target of motivational interviewing for an unhealthy behavior.

Explicit resistance/ambivalence ('I can't see quitting') signals the patient is NOT ready to change. The evidence-based response is motivational interviewing - acknowledge his perspective and explore ambivalence rather than confront.

clues.text: most appropriate statement by the physician
clues.note: The best statement uses OARS (open-ended questions, affirmations, reflections, summaries) to elicit the patient's OWN reasons for change. Asking about the downsides from his perspective is the motivational-interviewing move.

whatMatters: The patient is ambivalent and resistant. The motivational-interviewing approach is to acknowledge his reasons for drinking and ask an open-ended question that lets him voice the downsides himself: 'I can understand your reasons... What are the downsides to drinking from your perspective?' - answer C.
strategy: For 'most appropriate statement' counseling items with a resistant patient, choose motivational interviewing: remain nonjudgmental, reflect/affirm the patient's view, and ask OPEN-ENDED questions to elicit their own change talk. Avoid confrontation, lecturing, premature advice-giving, or prematurely 'giving up' on readiness.

options.text: "Alcohol is likely making your depression worse. It will be harder to treat your depression without addressing alcohol."
"Although you feel alcohol helps you to relax, I am concerned that you are using it to self-medicate your depression."
"I can understand your reasons for using alcohol. What are the downsides to drinking from your perspective?"
"I get it, but there are other, healthier ways to relax. Would you be willing to discuss those?"
"I understand your reasons and see that you are not ready to make a change. I am here to help when you feel ready."

eliminations.label: confrontational warning - raises resistance
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: directly telling him alcohol worsens his depression. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: confronting a resistant patient tends to increase defensiveness and 'sustain talk,' the opposite of motivational interviewing. You chose this. ✅ Correct if: the patient were already committed to change and asking for medical facts.

eliminations.label: expressing the doctor's concern - still doctor-centered
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: voicing the physician's worry about self-medication. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it imposes the clinician's interpretation rather than eliciting the patient's own motivation; not the open-ended, patient-centered MI move. ✅ Correct if: a more directive style were appropriate, but here MI is indicated.

eliminations.label: offering alternatives prematurely - skips ambivalence
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: suggesting other ways to relax. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: jumping to solutions before the patient has explored his own reasons to change is premature; MI first develops discrepancy/change talk. ✅ Correct if: the patient had already expressed readiness and wanted concrete strategies.

eliminations.label: prematurely conceding - abandons engagement
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: declaring he's 'not ready' and stepping back. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it disengages too early and forfeits the chance to elicit ambivalence; MI keeps the conversation going by exploring downsides. ✅ Correct if: the patient flatly refused any discussion after MI attempts.

reasoning: Resistant, ambivalent patient ('I can't see quitting')
Acknowledge his reasons + ask open-ended question to elicit his own change talk
'What are the downsides from your perspective?' -> answer C
mechanism: Motivational interviewing is a patient-centered, nonjudgmental method for strengthening a person's own motivation to change a maladaptive behavior, matched to their stage of readiness. Core techniques (OARS) are open-ended questions, affirmations, reflective listening, and summaries, used to develop discrepancy between current behavior and the patient's goals while rolling with resistance rather than confronting it. For an ambivalent patient who 'can't see quitting,' the most effective statement validates his reasons for drinking and invites him to articulate the downsides himself, eliciting 'change talk.' Confrontation, unsolicited advice, or prematurely conceding all undermine engagement.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Psychiatry > Behavioral change/motivational interviewing: OARS, stages of change, roll with resistance, develop discrepancy, elicit change talk; avoid confrontation and premature advice.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The cue 'I can't see quitting' = not ready -> use MI. Pick the open-ended, validating question that lets the patient generate his own reasons.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on motivational interviewing, OARS, ambivalence/change talk, stages of change, rolling with resistance? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: You chose A - the confrontational, fact-based warning. With a resistant patient, that increases defensiveness. The MI move is to explore his perspective (C).

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Default for resistant patients: validate + open-ended question. Reserve direct advice for patients who are ready.

keywords.front: Resistant patient ('can't see quitting') - best technique

keywords.back: Roll with resistance; develop discrepancy; elicit change talk

keywords.front: What to avoid with an ambivalent patient
keywords.back: Confrontation, lecturing, premature advice, giving up
Patient already in action/preparation stage and asking for guidance

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry: patient-centered counseling, OARS, stages of change.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry: patient-centered counseling, OARS, stages of change.

firstAid.detail: Psych: alcohol as a depressant, screening, brief intervention.
firstAid.location: Psych: alcohol as a depressant, screening, brief intervention.

firstAid.detail: Psych: open vs closed questions, reflective listening, avoiding confrontation.
firstAid.location: Psych: open vs closed questions, reflective listening, avoiding confrontation.

memoryHook: MI = let the patient argue for change. Don't push - ask 'what are the downsides for YOU?'
